segments.inferno-1-g69: This segment is only a full stop, closing the preceding question in the dialogue. It contains no words to parse.
segments.inferno-1-g70: “Or se’ tu” means “now are you”: “or” is an archaic/poetic form of “ora,” and “se’” is the contracted second-person singular of “sei.” “Quel Virgilio e quella fonte” uses demonstratives to mean “that famous Virgil” and metaphorically “that source”; the relative clause “che spandi...” refers chiefly to “fonte,” though it also praises Virgil. “Di parlar sì largo fiume” is an inverted poetic phrase: literally “so broad/copious a river of speech,” with “di parlar” depending on “fiume.”
segments.inferno-1-g71: The comma separates the quoted question just spoken from the following reporting clause. It marks the transition from direct speech to the narrator’s description of his own reply.
segments.inferno-1-g72: “Rispuos’” is an elided poetic form of “rispuosi,” the first-person singular passato remoto, “I replied.” The word order “rispuos’ io lui” is poetic; “lui” functions as the object of the reply, effectively “to him.” “Con vergognosa fronte” literally means “with an ashamed forehead,” where “fronte” stands for the face or countenance, expressing Dante’s bashful reverence.
segments.inferno-1-g64: This is a simple nominal predicate: “Poeta” stands before the verb for emphasis, and “fui” is the first-person singular past historic of “essere,” meaning “I was.” The speaker is identifying his former earthly role.
segments.inferno-1-g65: “E cantai di” means “and I sang of/about,” with “cantai” in the first-person singular past historic; in a poetic context it means composing epic verse about someone. “Quel giusto figliuol d’Anchise” is the object of the preposition “di,” referring to Aeneas, and “che venne di Troia” is a relative clause where “di” means “from.”
segments.inferno-1-g66: “Poi che” functions together as a temporal conjunction, “after” or “once,” even though literally it is formed from “then/afterwards” plus “that.” “Fu combusto” is a passive construction with “fu” as auxiliary plus the past participle “combusto,” and “’l” is the contracted article “il” before “superbo Ilïón.”
segments.inferno-1-g67: “Ma” marks a turn from the speaker’s self-identification to a direct question addressed to “tu.” “Perché” is interrogative, and “ritorni a tanta noia” means “turn back to so much trouble,” with “a” indicating the state or destination toward which Dante is returning.
segments.inferno-1-g68: This continues the question: “perché non sali” means “why do you not climb,” with “sali” as second-person singular present of “salire.” “Ch’è” is the contraction of “che è,” introducing a relative clause describing “il dilettoso monte”; “principio e cagion di tutta gioia” means that the mountain is the source and cause of all joy.
segments.inferno-1-g55: This segment contains only punctuation and has no grammatical or lexical content to translate.
segments.inferno-1-g56: “Rispuosemi” is an archaic/poetic single form combining the verb “rispuose” (“he answered”) with the enclitic object pronoun “mi” (“me”). The subject is understood from context, and the colon introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-1-g57: “Non omo” is elliptical: the verb “sono” is omitted, so the sense is “I am not a man.” “Omo” is an old form of modern Italian “uomo.”
segments.inferno-1-g58: “Omo già fui” supplies the contrast to the previous elliptical phrase: “I once was a man.” The word order is poetic, placing “omo” before the verb “fui,” the first-person singular preterite of “essere.”
segments.inferno-1-g59: “Li parenti miei” uses the old plural article “li” for modern “i,” and “parenti” here means “parents,” not merely relatives. “Furon” is a shortened poetic form of “furono,” the third-person plural preterite of “essere.”
segments.inferno-1-g60: This phrase continues the predicate from the previous segment: his parents were Lombards, specifically “mantoani,” Mantuan, “per patrïa,” that is, by homeland or native place. “Ambedui” means “both” and refers back to the two parents.
segments.inferno-1-g61: “Nacqui” is the first-person singular preterite, “I was born.” “Sub Iulio” uses the Latin preposition “sub” instead of Italian “sotto,” meaning “under Julius,” i.e. during Julius Caesar’s time or rule.
segments.inferno-1-g62: “Ancor che” functions as a concessive conjunction, “although” or “even though.” “Fosse” is the imperfect subjunctive of “essere,” used after the concessive expression, and “tardi” makes the clause impersonal: “although it was late.”
segments.inferno-1-g63: “Vissi” is the first-person singular preterite, coordinated with “nacqui”: “I lived.” “A Roma” means “in Rome,” while “sotto ’l buono Augusto” uses the contraction “’l” for “il” and means “under the good Augustus”; “nel tempo de li dèi falsi e bugiardi” uses archaic “de li” for modern “degli,” referring to the pagan age of false, lying gods.
segments.inferno-1-g1: “Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita” is a fronted prepositional phrase: “in the middle of the journey of our life,” with “cammin” as an apocopated poetic form of “cammino.” “Mi ritrovai” is first-person singular passato remoto with reflexive “mi,” meaning “I found myself”; “per una selva oscura” uses “per” in the sense of moving through or being within a place.
segments.inferno-1-g2: “Ché” is causal, introducing the reason for the speaker’s condition: “for/because.” “La diritta via” is the subject, with adjective before noun in a solemn poetic order; “era smarrita” is imperfect of “essere” plus a feminine singular past participle, giving the sense “had been lost” or “was lost.”
segments.inferno-1-g3: “Ahi quanto” opens an exclamation: “Alas, how…!” The phrase “a dir qual era” means “to tell what it was like,” with “dir” as a shortened infinitive and “qual era” as an indirect question. “Esta selva…” restates and describes the forest with three coordinated adjectives, and “che nel pensier rinova la paura” is a relative clause: the forest “renews fear” even in thought or memory.
segments.inferno-1-g4: “Tant’ è amara che…” is a result construction: “so bitter is it that…,” with “Tant’” elided from “tanto.” “Poco è più morte” is compressed poetic syntax: literally “death is little more,” meaning death is only slightly more bitter, or scarcely worse.
segments.inferno-1-g5: “Ma” turns from the terror of the forest to the narrator’s purpose. “Per trattar del ben” means “in order to treat/speak of the good,” with “trattar” as a shortened infinitive; “ch’i’ vi trovai” contracts “che io,” and “vi” means “there,” referring back to the forest or the experience within it.
segments.inferno-1-g6: “Dirò” is the future first-person singular of “dire”: “I will tell.” “De l’altre cose” is an archaic/poetic spelling for “delle altre cose,” here meaning “of/about the other things.” In “ch’i’ v’ho scorte,” “ch’i’” is “che io,” “v’ho” is “vi ho,” and “scorte” is an old feminine plural past participle agreeing with “cose,” meaning “seen/perceived there.”
segments.inferno-1-g7: The subject pronoun "Io" is explicit for emphasis, while "non so ben ridir" means literally "I do not know well how to recount," with "sapere" plus an infinitive expressing ability. "com’ i’ v’intrai" contracts "come io vi intrai": "vi" is an adverbial pronoun meaning "there," referring back to the dark wood.
segments.inferno-1-g8: "tant’ era pien di sonno" is a result construction: "so full of sleep was I," with poetic inversion placing the adjective phrase before the implied subject. "a quel punto" means "at that moment," and "che" introduces the consequence or defining moment: he abandoned "la verace via," the true path.
segments.inferno-1-g9: "Ma poi ch’i’" means "But after/when I," with elision of "che" and "io." "fui ... giunto" is literally "was arrived," using "essere" plus a past participle; English naturally renders it as "had reached" or "had arrived at."
segments.inferno-1-g10: "là dove" means "there where," locating the foot of the hill at the end of the valley. "che m’avea ... il cor compunto" is a relative clause referring to "valle"; "avea" is the archaic imperfect form of "aveva," used with the past participle "compunto" to form a pluperfect sense, "had pierced."
segments.inferno-1-g11: "guardai in alto e vidi" gives two coordinated simple past actions: "I looked upward and saw." "le sue spalle" personifies the hill as having "shoulders," meaning its slopes, and "vestite ... de’ raggi" is a past participial phrase meaning "clothed/covered with the rays." The final relative clause "che mena..." refers to "pianeta," here the sun, which metaphorically leads people straight along every path.
segments.inferno-1-g12: "Allor" marks the next moment in the narrative, "then." "fu ... queta" uses "fu" as a simple past copula, literally "was calm," while "un poco" modifies the adjective: the fear became only somewhat quieted.
segments.inferno-1-g13: "che" is a relative pronoun referring back to "la paura": it is the fear that had lasted. "m’era durata" uses "essere" as the auxiliary with "durata," giving a pluperfect sense, "had lasted for me/in me." "la notte ch’i’ passai" means "the night that I passed/spent," and "pieta" here has the older poetic sense of anguish or distress rather than religious piety.
segments.inferno-1-g41: This opens a correlative simile: qual anticipates tal in the later clause, meaning “as ... so.” quei is an archaic/poetic form for “that one, the man,” and che introduces the relative clause “who gladly gains.”
segments.inferno-1-g42: The subject is ’l tempo, with the article elided from il; giugne is an archaic spelling of giunge, “comes,” though that sense is not among the supplied options. che is relative, referring to “the time,” and face is the old form of fa, here causative: “makes him lose it.”
segments.inferno-1-g43: This che is consecutive, “so that,” completing the effect of the time that makes the man lose what he gained. ’n is an elision of in, not the letter name, and s’attrista is reflexive: “he saddens himself,” that is, “he becomes grieved.”
segments.inferno-1-g44: tal answers the earlier qual and means “so, in such a way”: the main comparison now arrives. mi is the direct object of fece, and la bestia sanza pace is the subject; sanza is an archaic form of senza, “without.”
segments.inferno-1-g45: che is a relative pronoun referring back to la bestia. It introduces the following descriptive clauses explaining how the beast affected Dante.
segments.inferno-1-g46: venendomi is a gerund with the object pronoun mi attached: “coming to me.” ’ncontro is an elided form of incontro, here meaning “toward” or “against,” so the phrase describes the beast advancing upon him.
segments.inferno-1-g47: a poco a poco is the idiom “little by little.” ripigneva is an imperfect form meaning “was pushing/driving back,” and là dove ’l sol tace means “there where the sun is silent,” with ’l for il; sol means “sun” here, a sense not listed among the options.
segments.inferno-1-g48: Mentre ch’ is a poetic form of the temporal conjunction, with che used pleonastically after mentre: “while.” i’ is the shortened form of io, and rovinava is imperfect, describing an ongoing fall or downward plunge.
segments.inferno-1-g49: dinanzi a li occhi means “before the eyes,” with a li as an older equivalent of modern agli. mi si fu offerto is a reflexive/passive-style construction: literally “offered/presented himself to me,” naturally “appeared to me.” chi introduces the person who appeared, and parea is the archaic imperfect of parere, “seemed”; fioco suggests faintness, perhaps of voice or presence, caused by lungo silenzio.
segments.inferno-1-g50: Quando introduces the temporal clause, and vidi is the first-person singular passato remoto, “I saw.” costui points to the figure just mentioned, and nel gran diserto means “in the great wilderness,” with diserto functioning as a noun here.
segments.inferno-1-g51: Miserere is a Latin imperative formula meaning “have mercy,” preserved directly in Dante’s Italian line. di me is the complement governed by Miserere, literally “of me,” idiomatically “on me.”
segments.inferno-1-g52: gridai is passato remoto, “I cried out” or “I shouted.” a lui marks the addressee of the cry: “to him.”
segments.inferno-1-g53: qual che forms a concessive indefinite expression: “whatever” or “whoever.” sii is the present subjunctive of essere after this expression, giving “whatever you may be.”
segments.inferno-1-g54: od ... od is the repeated form of o ... o, “whether ... or.” ombra means a shade or spirit, while omo is an archaic form of uomo, “man,” and certo here means “real” or “actual.”
segments.inferno-1-g30: “Temp’” is an elided form of “Tempo,” and the imperfect “era” sets the background: “it was the time.” “Dal principio del mattino” literally means “from the beginning of the morning,” i.e. just after dawn or early morning.
segments.inferno-1-g31: “’l sol” means “il sole,” the sun; “montava ’n sù” uses the imperfect to describe its ongoing rising. The relative “ch’eran” refers to “quelle stelle,” and “quando l’amor divino mosse di prima” looks back to creation: “when divine Love first moved/set in motion” the heavens, with “di prima” meaning “at first” or “for the first time.”
segments.inferno-1-g32: “Sì ch’” introduces a result: the preceding favorable signs made him hopeful. The subject of “m’era cagione” comes late—“l’ora del tempo e la dolce stagione”—and “a bene sperar di quella fiera” means “a cause for me to hope well concerning that beast”; “a la gaetta pelle” describes the beast as having a mottled or spotted hide.
segments.inferno-1-g33: “Ma non sì che...” qualifies the hope just mentioned: not so much hope that fear was excluded. “Desse” is an imperfect subjunctive of “dare,” used after the result construction, and “la vista che m’apparve d’un leone” means the sight/appearance of a lion that presented itself to him.
segments.inferno-1-g34: “Questi” refers back to the lion and means “this one.” “Parea che ... venisse” is an imperfect of seeming followed by an imperfect subjunctive, a common construction for appearance or impression; “con la test’ alta” has an elided “testa” and describes the lion’s proud posture.
segments.inferno-1-g35: “Sì che” again marks result: the lion’s threatening presence is so intense that even the air seems to shake. “Tremesse” is an imperfect subjunctive after “parea che,” and “ne” means roughly “because of it/him” or “at it,” referring to the lion.
segments.inferno-1-g36: “Ed una lupa” introduces a third beast, with “Ed” continuing the sequence. The noun “lupa” is feminine, literally “she-wolf,” and the rest of the sentence will resume it emphatically with “questa.”
segments.inferno-1-g37: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to the she-wolf. “Sembiava” is an archaic form of “sembrava,” and “carca di tutte brame” means “laden/full of every desire”; “ne la sua magrezza” is the older separated spelling of “nella,” “in her leanness.”
segments.inferno-1-g38: This second relative-style description continues the characterization of the she-wolf. “Fé” is a shortened past tense of “fece,” “made/caused,” followed by the infinitive “viver”; “grame” agrees with “molte genti” and means that many peoples lived wretchedly.
segments.inferno-1-g39: “Questa” emphatically resumes “una lupa” after the intervening relative clauses. “Mi porse tanto di gravezza” literally says “brought/offered me so much heaviness,” meaning imposed a great burden on him; “ch’uscia di sua vista” is a relative clause modifying “paura,” the fear that came forth from her appearance.
segments.inferno-1-g40: “Ch’io” introduces the result of “tanto”: so much burden that he lost hope. “Perdei” is a first-person simple past, and “de l’altezza” is the older separated form of “dell’altezza,” referring to the high place or height he had hoped to reach.
segments.inferno-1-g14: This opens a simile with “E come,” “And as/just as.” “Quei che” is an archaic-poetic way of saying “one who,” and “con lena affannata” is a prepositional phrase meaning “with labored breath,” with the adjective following the noun.
segments.inferno-1-g15: “Uscito” is a past participle agreeing with “quei,” giving a circumstantial phrase: “having emerged.” “Fuor del pelago” means “out of/from the open sea,” and “a la riva” uses the older separated form of modern “alla riva,” “to the shore.”
segments.inferno-1-g16: The subject is still “quei”: “si volge” is reflexive, “turns himself/turns.” “A l’acqua perigliosa” means “toward the dangerous water,” and “guata” is a present-tense verb, “gazes/looks intently,” completing the first half of the simile.
segments.inferno-1-g17: “Così” introduces the corresponding main clause of the comparison: “so/thus.” “L’animo mio” is the subject, with possessive adjective after the noun in normal Italian literary order: “my soul/mind.”
segments.inferno-1-g18: “Ch’” is elided “che,” introducing a relative clause referring to “l’animo mio.” “Ancor fuggiva” uses the imperfect tense to describe an ongoing state: the soul “was still fleeing.”
segments.inferno-1-g19: “Si volse” is the passato remoto of “volgersi,” “turned,” matching the earlier “si volge” in the simile. “A retro” means “back/backward,” and “a rimirar” is an infinitive of purpose, “to look back at”; “che non lasciò già mai persona viva” is a relative clause modifying “lo passo,” with “non ... mai” giving the force “never.”
segments.inferno-1-g20: “Poi ch’” means “after,” from “poi che,” and governs the completed action “èi posato.” “Èi” is an archaic first-person form meaning “I had,” so “èi posato ... il corpo lasso” means “I had rested my weary body”; “un poco” is adverbial, “a little.”
segments.inferno-1-g21: “Ripresi” is passato remoto, first person singular: “I resumed.” “Via” functions as the object, “my way/journey,” and “per la piaggia diserta” gives the route or terrain, “through/along the deserted slope.”
segments.inferno-1-g22: “Sì che” means “so that,” introducing a result clause. “’L piè fermo” is “the firm/planted foot,” and “era ’l più basso” means “was the lower one,” with “più basso” used comparatively.
segments.inferno-1-g23: “Ed ecco” is an abrupt narrative formula: “And behold” or “And there appeared.” It introduces the sudden appearance of the animal that follows.
segments.inferno-1-g24: This prepositional phrase locates the apparition: “almost at the beginning of the ascent.” “Al” is “a + il,” “at the,” “cominciar” is a nominalized infinitive meaning “beginning,” and “de l’erta” is the older separated form of modern “dell’erta.”
segments.inferno-1-g25: This is the noun phrase introduced by “ecco”: “a lonza,” traditionally a leopard or leopard-like beast. The adjectives “leggera” and “presta” follow and describe it as nimble and quick, while “molto” intensifies “presta.”
segments.inferno-1-g26: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to “lonza.” “Era coverta” is an imperfect passive construction, with archaic “coverta” for modern “coperta,” and “di pel macolato” indicates what covered it: “with/of spotted fur.”
segments.inferno-1-g27: “Non mi si partia” uses the reflexive verb “partirsi,” here in the imperfect: “it did not withdraw/depart from me.” “Dinanzi al volto” means “before/in front of the face,” with “al” governed by “dinanzi a.”
segments.inferno-1-g28: “Anzi” corrects or strengthens the previous negative: not only did it not move away, “rather” it hindered him. “’Mpediva” is an elided form of “impediva,” imperfect tense, and “tanto” prepares the result clause that follows: “so much that...”
segments.inferno-1-g29: “Ch’i’” is “che io,” “that I,” completing the “tanto ... che” result construction. “Fui per ritornar” is an idiom meaning “I was on the point of returning,” and “più volte vòlto” adds that he was turned back many times, with “vòlto” as a past participle meaning “turned.”
segments.inferno-1-g113: “A le quai” is the archaic separated form of modern “alle quali,” meaning “to whom/to which,” referring back to the blessed souls mentioned just before. “Se tu vorrai salire” is a future condition, “if you will wish to ascend,” with “poi” meaning “afterward/then.”
segments.inferno-1-g114: “Fia” is an archaic future form of “essere,” equivalent to “sarà,” “will be.” The phrase “a ciò” means “for that purpose,” and “più di me degna” places the comparative after the noun: “more worthy than I.”
segments.inferno-1-g115: “Con lei” means “with her,” referring to the more worthy soul just mentioned. “Ti lascerò” is future, “I shall leave you,” and “nel mio partire” is a nominalized infinitive phrase meaning “at my departure” or “when I depart.”
segments.inferno-1-g116: “Ché” introduces the reason for Virgil’s limitation: “for/because.” “Quello imperador” is a reverential periphrasis for God, and “che là sù regna” is a relative clause, “who reigns up there,” with “là sù” referring to Heaven.
segments.inferno-1-g117: “Perch’” here means “because/since,” not “why.” “I’ fu’” is the contracted poetic form of “io fui,” “I was,” and “ribellante a la sua legge” means “rebellious against his law,” with “a” marking the object toward which the rebellion is directed.
segments.inferno-1-g118: “Non vuol che” takes a subjunctive clause: “he does not want that...” “’n sua città” is a contraction of “in sua città,” and “si vegna” is impersonal subjunctive, “one should come”; “per me” means “through me” or “by my agency,” that is, under Virgil’s guidance.
segments.inferno-1-g119: “In tutte parti” means “in all places/everywhere,” with “parti” used broadly for regions or places. “Impera” and “regge” are coordinated present-tense verbs: God “holds empire” universally, and specifically “governs” there, in Heaven.
segments.inferno-1-g120: “Quivi” again means “there,” pointing to Heaven. “È” has the locative sense “is located,” and the two subjects are joined by “e”: “his city” and “the lofty throne,” with “l’alto seggio” referring to God’s exalted seat or throne.
segments.inferno-1-g121: This is an exclamatory clause: “oh felice” means “oh happy/fortunate.” “Colui cu’ ivi elegge” means “the one whom he chooses there/for there”; “cu’” is poetic for “cui,” the object of “elegge,” whose implied subject is God.
segments.inferno-1-g122: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the end of the preceding direct speech.
segments.inferno-1-g73: This is a vocative address to Virgil: “O” is the exclamatory “oh,” not the conjunction “or.” “De li” is an archaic separated form of modern “degli,” meaning “of the,” and “onore e lume” are two predicate-like nouns in apposition, naming Virgil as the honor and guiding light of other poets.
segments.inferno-1-g74: “Vagliami” is an optative/subjunctive-like form meaning “may it avail me,” with the enclitic pronoun “mi” attached; its subject is the following compound phrase “’l lungo studio e ’l grande amore.” The relative clause “che m’ha fatto cercar lo tuo volume” modifies especially “amore”: “che” is “which/that,” “m’ha fatto cercar” is a causative perfect construction, and “cercar” is an apocopated infinitive; “lo tuo” is an archaic article-plus-possessive form.
segments.inferno-1-g75: “Tu se’” is an archaic/poetic form of “tu sei,” the second-person singular present, “you are.” “Lo mio maestro” and “’l mio autore” use the old article before a possessive adjective, literally “the my,” but naturally “my”; both nouns are predicate complements of “se’.”
segments.inferno-1-g76: The repeated “tu se’” intensifies the praise, while “solo” means “only”: “you alone are.” “Colui da cu’ io tolsi” means “the one from whom I took,” with “cu’” as shortened “cui”; “tolsi” is passato remoto, and “lo bello stilo” is the object. The relative clause “che m’ha fatto onore” modifies “stilo”; “fare onore” is an idiom meaning “to do honor to” or “bring honor to.”
segments.inferno-1-g77: “Vedi” is a second-person singular imperative, “see” or “behold,” with “la bestia” as its direct object. “Per cu’” is shortened from “per cui,” here meaning “because of which” or “on account of which,” and “mi volsi” is the passato remoto of the reflexive “volgersi,” “I turned myself/turned back.”
segments.inferno-1-g78: “Aiutami” is the imperative “aiuta” plus the enclitic object pronoun “mi,” meaning “help me.” “Da lei” literally means “from her,” but in this request it has the force “against her” or “away from her,” referring back to the beast.
segments.inferno-1-g79: This is a vocative phrase addressed to Virgil. The adjective “famoso” modifies “saggio,” so the phrase means “renowned sage,” with the adjective placed before/alongside the noun in elevated style.
segments.inferno-1-g80: “Ch’” is elided “ché/che” introducing the reason for the plea: “because.” “Ella” refers grammatically to the feminine “bestia,” and “mi fa tremar” is a causative construction, “makes tremble”; “tremar” is an apocopated infinitive. With body parts, Italian uses the definite articles “le” and “i” where English naturally uses a possessive: “my veins and pulses.”
segments.inferno-1-g81: A te is a dative construction, literally “to you,” used with convien to mean “it is necessary for you.” Convien is an archaic/poetic form of conviene, and tenere altro vïaggio means “to follow/take another journey or path”; the infinitive tenere depends on convien.
segments.inferno-1-g82: Rispuose is an archaic preterite form meaning “he answered,” equivalent to modern rispose. It interrupts the quoted speech and identifies Virgil as the speaker.
segments.inferno-1-g83: Poi che functions together as “after” or “when.” In lagrimar mi vide, the infinitive lagrimar is governed by the perception verb vide: literally “he saw me weep,” with mi as the object of vide and the understood subject of the infinitive.
segments.inferno-1-g84: Se introduces the condition attached to the earlier command: “if you want to escape.” Vuo’ is a contracted poetic form of vuoi, and campar d’esto loco means “escape/survive from this place,” with d’ meaning “from” and esto an archaic form of questo.
segments.inferno-1-g85: Ché introduces the reason for needing another path: “for/because.” Questa bestia refers back to the frightening beast mentioned before and is the grammatical subject of the following verbs.
segments.inferno-1-g86: Per la qual is a relative phrase meaning “because of which” or “on account of which,” referring to the beast. Tu gride is an archaic second-person form for modern tu gridi, “you cry out.”
segments.inferno-1-g87: Non lascia altrui passar means “does not let anyone/others pass,” with passar as a shortened infinitive of passare after lascia. Per la sua via means “through/along her way,” where sua refers to the beast.
segments.inferno-1-g88: Ma contrasts the weaker idea “she does not let pass” with the stronger result: she obstructs the person until death. Tanto ... che is a result construction, “so much ... that”; lo and l’ are object pronouns referring to the person trying to pass.
segments.inferno-1-g89: E continues the description of the beast. Ha natura means “has a disposition/nature,” and sì malvagia e ria sets up the following result clause introduced by che: “so wicked and evil that...”
segments.inferno-1-g90: Che introduces the result of the beast’s evil nature. Mai non is a poetic negative combination meaning “never,” and empie la bramosa voglia means “fills/satisfies her greedy desire.”
segments.inferno-1-g91: Dopo ’l pasto uses ’l as a contraction of il, “the.” Ha più fame che pria is a comparison: “has more hunger than before,” with pria an archaic/poetic word for “before.”
segments.inferno-1-g92: Molti son li animali has poetic word order: the adjective molti is placed first, followed by the verb and subject, meaning “many are the animals.” A cui s’ammoglia means “with/to whom she joins herself in marriage,” with s’ as a reflexive pronoun and ammoglia used metaphorically for mating or allying herself.
segments.inferno-1-g93: E più saranno ancora continues the thought: “and more will still be.” Saranno is future tense, and più stands substantively, meaning “more [of them].”
segments.inferno-1-g94: Infin che means “until,” introducing the endpoint of the beast’s multiplying alliances. Verrà is future tense, “will come,” and its subject is ’l veltro, “the greyhound.”
segments.inferno-1-g95: Che is a relative pronoun referring to the veltro, “who.” La refers back to the beast, and farà morir is a causative construction: “will make/cause her to die”; con doglia adds the manner, “with pain.”
segments.inferno-1-g96: Questi refers to the veltro just mentioned and means “this one.” Non ciberà is future tense; here cibare is used as “feed on,” and terra né peltro means “land nor pewter,” with peltro often understood figuratively as wealth or money.
segments.inferno-1-g97: Ma contrasts what the veltro will not feed on with what he will feed on instead. Sapïenza is the first item in a three-part list of spiritual or moral sustenance.
segments.inferno-1-g98: Amore e virtute complete the list begun with sapïenza. Virtute is an archaic/poetic form of virtù, and together the three nouns describe the veltro’s non-material nourishment.
segments.inferno-1-g99: E sua nazion sarà adds another prophecy about the veltro. Nazion here means “birth” or “origin,” and tra feltro e feltro is deliberately enigmatic; read literally, it means “between felt and felt,” suggesting humble cloth or an obscure place of origin.
segments.inferno-1-g100: “Di quella umile Italia” depends on “salute”: literally “the salvation of that lowly/low-lying Italy.” “Fia” is an archaic future form of “sarà,” and “per cui” means “for which”; the verb “morì” is singular because the first named subject is “la vergine Cammilla,” with the other names continuing in the next segment.
segments.inferno-1-g101: “Eurialo e Turno e Niso” adds further subjects to “morì,” although the verb remains singular by poetic coordination after the first subject. “Di ferute” is an archaic plural expression meaning “from/by wounds,” explaining the manner of their deaths.
segments.inferno-1-g102: “Questi” means “this one,” referring to the expected savior mentioned just before. “La” is the direct object, referring to the she-wolf, and “per ogne villa” means “through every village/town,” with “ogne” an old form of “ogni.”
segments.inferno-1-g103: “Fin che” forms the conjunction “until.” “L’avrà rimessa” is future perfect, “will have sent her back,” with “l’” standing for “la”; “ne lo ’nferno” is the older separated form of modern “nell’inferno,” with apocope in “’nferno.”
segments.inferno-1-g104: “Là onde” means “there whence/from where,” not literally “there wave”; here “onde” is a relative adverb. “’Nvidia” is an elided form of “invidia,” and “dipartilla” contracts “dipartì + la,” meaning “sent her forth/separated her from there,” with “la” again referring to the she-wolf.
segments.inferno-1-g105: “Ond’” is elided “onde,” here meaning “therefore.” “Per lo tuo me’” means “for your better good,” with “me’” an apocopated form of “meglio,” not the pronoun “me”; “che tu mi segui” depends on “penso e discerno” and has the force “that you follow/should follow me.”
segments.inferno-1-g106: This coordinated clause promises the speaker’s role: “and I will be your guide.” “Sarò” is the future of “essere,” and “tua guida” is a predicate noun phrase, literally “your guide.”
segments.inferno-1-g107: “Trarrotti” is a poetic contraction of “trarrò ti,” meaning “I will draw/lead you.” “Di qui” means “from here,” and “per loco etterno” means “through an eternal place,” with “loco” and “etterno” archaic forms of “luogo” and “eterno.”
segments.inferno-1-g108: “Ove” introduces a relative place clause referring to the “loco etterno”: “where.” “Udirai” is future second singular, and “le disperate strida” is the object, “the hopeless/desperate shrieks.”
segments.inferno-1-g109: “Vedrai” is another future second singular coordinated with “udirai.” “Li” is an archaic masculine plural article, and “antichi spiriti dolenti” means “the ancient sorrowing spirits”; the supplied sense list for “spiriti” does not match the noun meaning here.
segments.inferno-1-g110: “Ch’” is a relative pronoun referring back to “li antichi spiriti.” “A la seconda morte” means “for/to the second death,” and “ciascun grida” uses singular “each one cries out,” emphasizing each spirit individually.
segments.inferno-1-g111: This begins a new coordinated object of “vederai”: “you will see those who are content in the fire.” “Color” is an apocopated form of “coloro,” “those,” “son” is shortened “sono,” and “nel foco” is archaic/poetic for “nel fuoco.”
segments.inferno-1-g112: “Perché” gives the reason why they are content: “because they hope.” “Speran” is shortened “sperano,” followed by “di” plus infinitive; “quando che sia” is an indefinite temporal phrase meaning “whenever it may be,” and “a le beate genti” means “to/among the blessed people.”
segments.inferno-1-g123: This is an elliptical speech tag: the verb of saying is omitted, so it means “And I said to him.” The prepositional phrase “a lui” marks the addressee, “to him.”
segments.inferno-1-g124: “Poeta” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil as “Poet.” It stands outside the syntax of the following request.
segments.inferno-1-g125: “Io ti richeggio” means “I beg/request you”; here “richeggio” is an archaic or poetic form related to “richiedere,” not the listed verb meaning “abound.” “Per quello Dio” is an oath-like appeal, “by that God,” and “che tu non conoscesti” is a relative clause with “conoscesti” in the passato remoto, “whom you did not know.”
segments.inferno-1-g126: “Acciò ch’” introduces a purpose clause, “so that.” The verb “fugga” is present subjunctive, first person singular, used because the clause expresses Dante’s desired purpose: that he may escape the present evil and something worse.
segments.inferno-1-g127: This “che” introduces the content of the request: Dante asks Virgil “that you lead me.” “Meni” is present subjunctive, second person singular, dependent on “richeggio,” and “là dov’ or dicesti” means “there where you just now said,” with “dov’” shortened from “dove.”
segments.inferno-1-g128: “Sì ch’” introduces a result or purpose clause, “so that,” and “veggia” is an archaic subjunctive form of “vedere,” “that I may see,” not the listed verb “keep vigil.” “Color” is a shortened form of “coloro,” “those,” followed by the relative “cui,” and “tu fai cotanto mesti” means “you make/represent as so very sorrowful.”
segments.inferno-1-g129: “Allor” is a narrative time adverb, “then.” “Si mosse” is passato remoto of “muoversi,” “he moved/set out”; the listed senses for “mosse” do not match this verb form.
segments.inferno-1-g130: “E io” coordinates Dante’s action with Virgil’s. “Li” is an archaic dative pronoun meaning “to him/after him,” and “tenni dietro” is an idiom with “tenni,” passato remoto of “tenere,” meaning “I followed behind.”

sentences.inferno-1-s23: “Now are you that Virgil, and that source which pours forth so broad a river of speech?”
sentences.inferno-1-s24: I replied to him with an ashamed face.
sentences.inferno-1-s19: I was a poet, and I sang of that righteous son of Anchises who came from Troy, after proud Ilium was burned.
sentences.inferno-1-s20: But why do you turn back to so much trouble?
sentences.inferno-1-s21: Why do you not climb the delightful mountain, which is the origin and cause of all joy?
sentences.inferno-1-s17: He answered me: “Not a man; once I was a man, and my parents were Lombards, both Mantuan by homeland.
sentences.inferno-1-s18: I was born under Julius, although it was late, and I lived in Rome under good Augustus, in the time of the false and lying gods.
sentences.inferno-1-s1: Midway along the journey of our life, I found myself in a dark forest, for the straight way had been lost.
sentences.inferno-1-s2: Alas, how hard a thing it is to tell what that forest was like, this forest wild and rough and severe, which renews fear in one’s thought!
sentences.inferno-1-s3: So bitter is it that death is scarcely more so; but, in order to speak of the good that I found there, I will tell of the other things that I saw there.
sentences.inferno-1-s4: I cannot clearly recount how I entered there, so full of sleep was I at that moment when I forsook the true way.
sentences.inferno-1-s5: But after I had reached the foot of a hill, there where that valley ended which had pierced my heart with fear, I looked upward and saw its slopes already clothed with the rays of the sun, which leads people straight along every path.
sentences.inferno-1-s6: Then the fear was somewhat quieted, which had lasted in the lake of my heart through the night that I passed in such distress.
sentences.inferno-1-s13: And as one who gladly gains, and then the time comes that causes him to lose, so that in all his thoughts he weeps and grieves, so did the beast without peace make me: coming toward me, little by little it drove me back to where the sun is silent.
sentences.inferno-1-s14: While I was plunging down into a low place, before my eyes there appeared to me one who, through long silence, seemed faint.
sentences.inferno-1-s15: When I saw this man in the great wilderness, “Have mercy on me,” I cried to him, “whatever you may be, whether shade or real man!”
sentences.inferno-1-s10: It was the time at the beginning of morning, and the sun was climbing upward with those stars that were with it when divine Love first set those beautiful things in motion; so the hour of the day and the sweet season gave me cause to hope well concerning that beast with the dappled hide; but not so much that the sight of a lion, which appeared to me, did not give me fear.
sentences.inferno-1-s11: This one seemed to be coming against me with its head held high and with ravenous hunger, so that the air seemed to tremble because of it.
sentences.inferno-1-s12: And a she-wolf, which in her leanness seemed laden with every craving, and had already made many peoples live in misery—this one brought upon me so much burden through the fear that came from her appearance that I lost hope of the height.
sentences.inferno-1-s7: And just as one who, with labored breath, having emerged from the open sea onto the shore, turns to the dangerous water and gazes, so my soul, which was still fleeing, turned back to look again at the pass that never yet left any person alive.
sentences.inferno-1-s8: After I had rested my weary body a little, I resumed my way along the deserted slope, so that the firm foot was always the lower one.
sentences.inferno-1-s9: And behold, almost at the beginning of the steep ascent, a leopard, nimble and very quick, covered with spotted fur; and it did not move away from before my face, but rather hindered my path so much that I was turned back to return many times.
sentences.inferno-1-s34: If afterward you wish to ascend to them, a soul more worthy than I will be there for that; with her I shall leave you when I depart, for that Emperor who reigns up there, because I was rebellious against his law, does not will that anyone come into his city through me.
sentences.inferno-1-s35: He holds empire in every place and governs there; there is his city and his lofty throne: oh, happy is the one whom he chooses for that place!
sentences.inferno-1-s25: “O honor and guiding light of the other poets, may the long study and the great love that made me search your book avail me.
sentences.inferno-1-s26: You are my master and my author; you alone are the one from whom I took the beautiful style that has brought me honor.
sentences.inferno-1-s27: See the beast because of which I turned back; help me against her, renowned sage, for she makes my veins and pulses tremble.”
sentences.inferno-1-s28: “You must take another road,” he answered, after he saw me weeping, “if you want to escape from this savage place; for this beast, because of which you cry out, lets no one pass along her way, but hinders him so much that she kills him; and she has a nature so wicked and evil that she never satisfies her greedy desire, and after feeding she has more hunger than before.”
sentences.inferno-1-s29: Many are the animals with whom she mates, and still more will there be, until the greyhound comes, who will make her die in pain.
sentences.inferno-1-s30: This one will not feed on land or money, but on wisdom, love, and virtue; and his birth will be between felt and felt.
sentences.inferno-1-s31: He shall be the salvation of that low-lying Italy for which the maiden Camilla, Euryalus, Turnus, and Nisus died from wounds.
sentences.inferno-1-s32: He will drive her through every village, until he has sent her back into Hell, there from where envy first sent her forth.
sentences.inferno-1-s33: Therefore, for your own good, I think and judge that you should follow me; and I will be your guide, and I will lead you from here through an eternal place, where you will hear the hopeless shrieks, you will see the ancient sorrowing spirits, each of whom cries out for the second death; and you will see those who are content in the fire, because they hope to come, whenever it may be, among the blessed people.
sentences.inferno-1-s37: And I to him: “Poet, I beg you, by that God whom you did not know, so that I may flee this evil and worse, that you lead me there where you just said, so that I may see the gate of Saint Peter and those whom you make so sorrowful.”
sentences.inferno-1-s38: Then he set out, and I followed behind him.
